“Sometimes an image isn’t blurry, yet it’s still difficult to read. In many cases, the problem is simply low contrast. Improving image contrast can make text much clearer, especially in OCR applications.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Histogram Equalization and CLAHEIn the previous article, we explored how Gaussian Blur, Median Blur, and Bilateral Filter reduce image noise.
However, noise is not always the problem.
Some images appear clean but still have poor contrast, causing the text to blend into the background.
In such cases, filtering alone is not enough.
Instead, the image contrast needs to be enhanced.
In this article, we’ll explore two powerful contrast enhancement techniques available in OpenCV:

• Histogram Equalization

• CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization)

What Is Contrast?
Contrast is the difference between the dark and bright regions of an image.
In a high-contrast image, objects and text stand out clearly from the background.
In a low-contrast image, pixel intensities are very similar, making details difficult to distinguish.
Examples of low-contrast images include:

• Faded text

• Documents captured under poor lighting

• Old scanned documents

• Medicine boxes with matte packaging
Low contrast can significantly reduce OCR accuracy.

What Is a Histogram?
A histogram is a graph that shows the distribution of pixel intensity values in an image.
Pixel intensity values range from:
0 --------------------------- 255Black White
A histogram helps us understand whether an image is:

• dark,

• bright,

• low contrast,

• or high contrast.
In simple terms, a histogram provides a summary of an image’s brightness distribution.

What Is Histogram Equalization?
Histogram Equalization is a technique that improves image contrast by redistributing pixel intensity values.
Its goal is to:

• brighten darker regions,

• balance brighter regions,

• spread intensity values over a wider range.
In OpenCV:
equalized = cv2.equalizeHist(gray)After applying Histogram Equalization, text and edges often become much more visible.

Does Histogram Equalization Always Work Well?
No.
Histogram Equalization applies the same transformation to the entire image.
As a result:

• some regions may become overly bright,

• image noise may become more noticeable,

• local image details may be lost.
It is most effective when the lighting is relatively uniform.

What Is CLAHE?
To overcome these limitations, CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) was developed.
Instead of processing the entire image at once, CLAHE divides the image into small regions (tiles) and enhances the contrast of each region independently.
These regions are then blended smoothly together.
In addition, the Contrast Limited mechanism prevents excessive contrast enhancement, reducing the risk of amplifying image noise.
In OpenCV:
clahe = cv2.createCLAHE(
clipLimit=2.0,
tileGridSize=(8,8)
)result = clahe.apply(gray)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Because real-world images often contain uneven lighting, CLAHE generally produces better results for OCR applications.

Common Mistakes
Some common mistakes include:
❌ Applying Histogram Equalization directly to a color image.
❌ Assuming CLAHE always produces the best result.
❌ Using a very high clipLimit, resulting in unnatural-looking images.
❌ Forgetting that increasing contrast can also increase visible noise.
The most appropriate method should always be selected according to the characteristics of the image.

What Have We Learned?
In this article, we explored two important techniques for improving image contrast.
First, we learned how Histogram Equalization redistributes pixel intensity values to increase overall image contrast. Then, we examined how CLAHE enhances contrast locally while preserving image details, making it particularly effective under uneven lighting conditions.
Choosing the right contrast enhancement technique not only improves the visual quality of an image but also helps OCR systems recognize text more accurately.
